coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot stabilized copper sulphide nanoparticles decorated graphene oxide hydrogel for high performance asymmetric supercapacitor
coredata.dc:creator: De, Bibekananda
Kuila, Tapas
Kim, Nam Hoon
Lee, Joong Hee
coredata.dc:description: A three-dimensional (3-D) porous structure was designed based on carbon dot (CD)-supported copper sulphide (CuS)-decorated graphene oxide (GO) hydrogel for using in a high-performance asymmetric supercapacitor device to improve the specific capacitance, cyclic stability and energy density of CuS as well as GO based supercapacitors. CD coated CuS (CuS@CD) decorated GO hydrogels (CuS@CD-GOH) were prepared by a simple hydrothermal reaction at 180 °C and optimized through different spectroscopic, diffraction, microscopic and electrochemical analyses. CD acted as a stabilizer for the CuS nanoparticles and help to bind strongly CuS nanoparticles with GO inside the 3-D hydrogel structure. The CuS@CD-GOH exhibited high specific capacitance of 920 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1. The optimal CuS@CD-GOH was used as a positive electrode for the fabrication of asymmetric supercapacitor along with reduced GO as the negative electrode, which delivered the highest energy density up to 28 W h kg−1 along with long cycling life and retains up to 90% specific capacitance after 5000 cycles. The results are excellent in comparison to the reported CuS and composite GO hydrogel based supercapacitors. Thus, this work will provide a new insight of CuS and GO based composite 3-D structures for supercapacitor applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene hydrogel
Carbon dot
CuS
Asymmetric supercapacitor
Energy density

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CD stabilized CuS nanoparticles (CuS@CD)
Preparation and characterization of the materials

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy
Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was supported by Basic Research Laboratory Program (2014R1A4A1008140) through the Ministry of Science, ICT & Future Planning of Korea and also was supported by the Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP) and the Ministry of Trade, Industry &Energy (MOTIE) of the Republic of Korea (No. 20164030201070).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FTIR spectra, (b) XRD patterns, (c) Raman spectra and (d) XPS spectra of the hydrogels. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) C 1s, (b) O 1s, (c) Cu 2p and (d) S 2p XPS spectra of CuS@CD-GOH. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
FE-SEM images of (a) GOH, (b) CuS-GOH, (c) CuS@CD-GOH at 2 KX resolution and (d) CuS@CD-GOH at 50 KX resolution. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

FE-SEM elemental color mapping of CuS@CD-GOH: (a) C; (b) O; (c) S; (d) Cu; (e) C, O, S and Cu. (f) HAADF-STEM image of CuS@CD-GOH and its corresponding elemental color mapping for (g) C, (h) O, (i) Cu and (j) S. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a,b) TEM, and (c,d) HRTEM images of CuS@CD-GOH. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Comparative CV curves of GOH, CuS-GOH and CuS@CD-GOH at 25 mV s−1 scan rate; (b) CV curves of CuS@CD-GOH at different scan rates; (c) GCD curves of CuS@CD-GOH at different current densities; (d) specific capacitance values and rate capabilities of CuS@CD-GOH at different current densities; (e) specific capacitance values (at 1 A g−1 current density) and (f) cyclic stability (at 5 A g−1 current density) of CuS@CD-GOH at different wt% of CuS@CD with respect to GO. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) CV curves of RGO and CuS@CD-GOH at a scan rate of 25 mV s−1; (b) CV curves of the asymmetric supercapacitor at different scan rates; (c) GCD curves of the asymmetric supercapacitor at different current densities; (d) specific capacitance values and rate capability of the asymmetric supercapacitor at different current densities; (e) Ragone plot of the corresponding energy and power densities for the asymmetric supercapacitor and (f) cyclic stability of the asymmetric supercapacitor up to 5000 cycles at a current density of 5 A g−1 (inset Fig. shows the GCD curves at 1st and 5000th cycles). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Nyquist plots of the 1st and 5000th cycles for the asymmetric supercapacitor. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Comparative electrochemical performance of different CuS and graphene oxide composite hydrogel based asymmetric supercapacitors with the present work.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot stabilized copper sulphide nanoparticles decorated graphene oxide hydrogel for high performance asymmetric supercapacitor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Materials Institute for BIN Convergence Technology (BK21 Plus Global), Department of BIN Convergence Technology, Chonbuk National University, Jeonju, Jeonbuk, 54896, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research-Central Mechanical Engineering Research Institute

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Carbon Composite Research Centre, Department of Polymer-Nano Science and Technology, Chonbuk National University, Jeonju, Jeonbuk, 54896, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Polymer-Nano Science and Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors. Advanced Materials Institute for BIN Convergence Technology (BK21 Plus Global), Department of BIN Convergence Technology, Chonbuk National University, Jeonju, Jeonbuk, 54896, Republic of Korea.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Advanced Materials Institute for BIN Convergence Technology (BK21 Plus Global)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A three-dimensional (3-D) porous structure was designed based on carbon dot (CD)-supported copper sulphide (CuS)-decorated graphene oxide (GO) hydrogel for using in a high-performance asymmetric supercapacitor device to improve the specific capacitance, cyclic stability and energy density of CuS as well as GO based supercapacitors. CD coated CuS (CuS@CD) decorated GO hydrogels (CuS@CD-GOH) were prepared by a simple hydrothermal reaction at 180 °C and optimized through different spectroscopic, diffraction, microscopic and electrochemical analyses. CD acted as a stabilizer for the CuS nanoparticles and help to bind strongly CuS nanoparticles with GO inside the 3-D hydrogel structure. The CuS@CD-GOH exhibited high specific capacitance of 920 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1. The optimal CuS@CD-GOH was used as a positive electrode for the fabrication of asymmetric supercapacitor along with reduced GO as the negative electrode, which delivered the highest energy density up to 28 W h kg−1 along with long cycling life and retains up to 90% specific capacitance after 5000 cycles. The results are excellent in comparison to the reported CuS and composite GO hydrogel based supercapacitors. Thus, this work will provide a new insight of CuS and GO based composite 3-D structures for supercapacitor applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitor is one of the most promising devices to store electrical energy for the next-generation wearable electronics due to the transport of high power within a very short period. Carbon based electrical double layer capacitors (EDLCs) including graphene-based materials are the most widely investigated as electrodes of supercapacitors [1–4], which possess dramatic advantages of high power density and excellent cycling stability due to their large specific surface area, superior elasticity, high chemical stability and excellent electrical conductivity [5–9]. However, graphene's propensity towards aggregation and restacking significantly affect the supercapacitor performance by reducing the ion-accessible surfaces and limiting ion and electron transport due to narrower channels. Therefore, a variety of graphene-based three-dimensional (3-D) porous materials, like hydrogel, aerogel, sponges, porous films, etc. have been extensively investigated to overcome these limitations through providing additional ion-accessible surface for charge storage and also facilitate the ion diffusion within the structure [10–14]. Several synthesis methods for 3D graphene have been reported to realize a wide range of pore morphologies from ultrafine (<100 nm) to macroscale (>1 μm) [15–18]. However, the specific capacitance values of these hydrogels are not up to the mark for advanced energy storage applications due to the finite electrical charge separation at the interface between the electrode materials and electrolyte.

On the other hand, pseudocapacitors based on red-ox-active materials like transition metal oxides or sulphides exhibit high specific capacitance and energy density because they employ fast and reversible surface red-ox reactions for charge storage [19–22]. However, their drawbacks associated with poor electrical conductivity, rate capability and cyclic stability due to the volume change in electrolyte solution during electrochemical analyses. Moreover, the preparation and stabilization of the metal-based nanoparticles with desire shape and size is very difficult. Therefore, current research is mainly focusing on the hybrid supercapacitors based on carbon and transition metal based hybrid nanostructures [23–26]. Based on these concepts, preparation of these metal based nanomaterials in presence of functionalized carbon based materials, as well as 3-D conversion of the hybrid system will enhance both the specific capacitance value and cyclic stability with desired shape and size of the nanoparticles. In this regards, carbon dot (CD), a highly functionalized quantum sized carbon nanomaterial is widely used as a surface stabilizer for metal nanoparticles [27,28]. Recently, composites of CD with transition metal nanoparticles and CD based hydrogels are also used in energy storage applications, as its surface functional groups facilitate the redox reaction with electrolyte and rise to an additional pseudo-capacitance contribution [26,27,29–34]. Due to the presence of CD in these composites, they also possess better conductivity because of improved crystallinity, which enhances the transport of electrons during particles, leading to improved rate capability [34]. Further, metal sulphides including copper sulphide (CuS) are cheap and environment friendly important semiconductor materials due to its abundance and low toxicity. Thus, they have recently emerged as promising electrode materials in energy storage applications [35]. Currently, CuS has been utilized as a potential material for supercapacitor applications [36–40]. However, low specific capacitance, poor rate capability and cyclic stability as well as low energy density are the big challenges for the CuS based supercapacitors, even though using composites with carbon-based conducting materials. Most of the CuS based supercapacitors exhibited <80% cyclic stability just after 1000–2000 cycles [36–38].

Therefore, the main aim of the present work is the preparation of a CD stabilized CuS nanoparticles with desired shape and size, and decoration of them into the graphene oxide (GO) hydrogel to get high performance supercapacitive material with high energy density, rate capability and cyclic stability. Herein, CD will also act as a good interacting agent with GO due to the presence of large number of surface functional groups. The present work also designed a high performance asymmetric supercapacitor device using the composite 3-D structure as positive electrode and reduced graphene oxide (RGO) as the negative electrode.

d-glucose and l-ascorbic acid (Purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Germany) were used as precursors for CD, while copper sulphate (CuSO4.5H2O) and sodium thiosulphate (Na2S2O3.5H2O) (Sigma-Aldrich) were used as CuS precursors. GO was prepared by modified Hummers method from natural graphite powder (Sigma-Aldrich) as reported in literature [41,42]. All other chemicals and reagents used in this study were reagent grade.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CD stabilized CuS nanoparticles (CuS@CD)
ce:section.ce:para: CuS@CD was prepared by a one-pot hydrothermal reaction at 180 °C for 12 h, using a teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave reactor. Before transferring into the autoclave, 4 × 10−4 mol (100 mg) of copper sulphate was dissolved in 25 mL distilled water with 8 × 10−4 mol (198.8 mg) of sodium thiosulphate. In addition, 2 × 10−3 mol (360 mg) of glucose was dissolved in 25 mL water with 2 × 10−4 mol (35 mg) of ascorbic acid. Both the solutions were mixed homogeneously by stirring and followed by transfer into the 100 mL autoclave. After completion of the hydrothermal reaction, the autoclave reactor was allowed to cool naturally. The prepared sample was washed with distilled water using repeated centrifugation and finally dispersed in water by sonication for 10 min. Pure CD (using 360 mg of glucose and 35 mg of ascorbic acid) and pure CuS (using 100 mg of copper sulphate and 198.8 mg of sodium thiosulphate) were also prepared by the same procedure for comparative study.

The hybrid hydrogel of GO with CuS@CD were prepared by the same hydrothermal reaction using teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave at 180 °C for 12 h. In this procedure, different amounts (25, 50 and 75 mg) of CuS@CD were dispersed separately with 100 mg of GO in 50 mL distilled water with the help of mechanical shearing and sonication at room temperature. Then, the solutions were transferred into teflon lined stainless steel autoclave before hydrothermal reaction. After completion of the reaction, it was cooled down to room temperature. Then the obtained GO hydrogels were washed with distilled water by submerging them into 1 L distilled water for 24 h. The prepared hydrogels were freeze-dried for 3 days in order to remove water without destroying their structure. For the comparison purpose CuS decorated GO hydrogel and pure GO based hydrogel were also prepared by similar procedure. The pure GO based, CuS and CuS@CD decorated GO hydrogels are coded as GOH, CuS-GOH and CuS@CD-GOH, respectively.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet 6700 spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA) using KBr pellets. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was carried out at room temperature on a X'pert Powder diffractometer (PANalytical, Netherlands) using CuKα radiation (0.154 nm) at a scan rate of 2° min−1. Raman studies of the samples were carried out by a Nanofinder 30 spectroscopy system (Tokyo Instruments Co., Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an AXIS-NOVA spectrometer (Kratos Analytical Ltd, Manchester, UK) to characterize the chemical structure of the materials. The structural morphology, and elemental analysis (EDS) of the materials were carried out by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) using a JSM-6701F microscope (JEOL, Japan) installed in the Center for University-Wide Research Facilities (CURF) at Chonbuk National University and the internal structure of the nanostructures were analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (FE-TEM) using a JEM-2200 FS microscope (JEOL, Japan) in Jeonju center of KBSI. The high angle annular dark field (HAADF) scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) with elemental mapping of the hydrogel sample was performed by a Titan G2 60–300 (FEI, USA) with low-background double tilt holder Super-X 4SDD EDS system (Bruker, Germany).

The electrochemical performance was carried out in both three-electrode and two-electrode systems at room temperature by using a CH660D electrochemical workstation (CH Instruments Inc., USA) as the methods were described in our previous reports [43,44]. Prior to analysis, samples were annealed at 500 °C for 2 h in an argon atmosphere. Cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanometric charge-discharge (GCD), and electrical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were carried out to characterize the electrochemical performance of the samples using 6 M KOH as the electrolyte at room temperature. The working electrodes were prepared using a mixture of as-prepared hydrogel powder as active materials; poly(vinylidene fluoride) (PVDF), and carbon black as binders with a weight ratio of 80: 10: 10 (active materials: PVDF: carbon black). Each working electrode contained about 2 mg of active material and had a geometric surface area of about 1 cm2. The typical three-electrode experimental cell was equipped with platinum wire and AgCl/Ag as the counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. Specific capacitance was calculated from Equations (1) And (2),
(1)
C
CV
= Δ q /
( v × Δ V × m )
where, CCV is the specific capacitance calculated from CV curves, Δq is obtained by integrating current within the potential window for a particular scan rate, v is the scan rate, ΔV is the potential window in the curve, and m is the mass deposited on the electrode.
(2)
C
GCD
=
( Δ t × I )
/
( Δ V × m )
where, CGCD is the specific capacitance calculated from GCD curves, I is the applied current for the charge-discharge measurement, Δt is the discharge time, m is the mass deposited on the electrode, and ΔV is the potential window in the curve.

The two electrode electrochemical performance of the asymmetric supercapacitor was evaluated using the CuS@CD-GOH as the positive electrode material and chemically reduced graphene oxide (RGO) as the negative electrode material. The asymmetric device was fabricated inside a stainless steel point-cell using 6 M aqueous KOH as the electrolyte as reported in our previous works [43,44]. The mass ratio of positive electrode and negative electrode was taken as 0.75 according to their CV curves using the following Equation (3),
(3)
m +
/m
−
=
(
C
-
× Δ
V -
)
/
(
C
+
× Δ
V +
)
where, m is the mass, C is the capacitance and ΔV is the potential window for positive (+) and negative (−) electrodes.

Based on the capacitance calculated from GCD curves, the energy density (E) and power density (P) of the asymmetric supercapacitor were calculated using Equations (4) And (5):
(4)
E = ½
C Δ
V 2
where, C is the capacitance and ΔV is the potential window of the asymmetric supercapacitor.
(5)
P = E/ Δ t
where, Δt is the discharge time of the asymmetric supercapacitor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the materials
ce:section.ce:para: CuS@CD was prepared through an in-situ hydrothermal reaction, in which copper sulphate and sodium thiosulphate were used for the source of CuS, and glucose was used as carbon precursor for the CD using ascorbic acid as the catalyst to promote hydrolysis, dehydration, condensation, polymerization, aromatization, and carbonization reactions [45,46]. Herein, in-situ formed CD was utilized as surface stabilizer for the CuS nanoparticles to prevent their aggregation by surface covering through fine CD particles with size below 5 nm, as shown in TEM image of CuS@CD in Fig. S1a. Fig. S1b shows the FE-SEM image of the nanohybrid. Both TEM and FE-SEM images indicate the formation of hierarchical CuS@CD nanohybrid. Whereas, Fig. S1c indicates that the large size agglomerated CuS nano-spheres were formed in absence of CD precursors. In absence of CuS sources, the dimension of CD was found to be around 3 nm with spherical shape, which shows lattice fringes of 0.23 nm for the d(100) graphitic spacing (Fig. S1d) [27]. The formation of CuS was confirmed from the XRD analyses of CuS@CD (Fig. S2) by comparing the results with standard JCPDS data (Card No. 060464 of CuS) and literature. The major diffraction peaks of CuS@CD were found at 2θ = 27.05, 27.68, 29.27, 31.78, 32.88, 47.93, 52.72, and 59.36°, corresponding to the d(100), d(101), d(102), d(103), d(106), d(110), d(108), and d(116) crystallographic planes of CuS [39,40,47]. The presence of carbon, oxygen, copper and sulphur in CuS@CD was further confirmed by EDAX analysis as shown in Fig. S3a. The nanohybrid was prepared using excess amount of carbon precursor with CuS source. The mole ratio of copper sulphate and glucose was taken 1:5 to prepare the final material. However, the actual amount of CD was consumed 3.6 to stabilize the CuS nanoparticle (by surface interactions) during preparation of CuS@CD, as observed from elemental analysis (Fig. S3a). The excess amount of CD was washed out during the purification of the final material. So, the actual mole ratio of copper sulphate and glucose is required 1:3.6 to obtain the final nanohybrid material. The atomic ratio of Cu:S was found approximately 1 in both CuS@CD and CuS, as shown in Figs. S3a and S3b.

The GO based hydrogels were prepared by similar hydrothermal reaction, in which GO was reduced to form RGO and further self-assembled into hydrogel driven by the π–π stacking interaction of graphene sheets under hydrothermal conditions. These self-assembled 3-D network structures absorbed lot of water molecules as they were prepared from aqueous solution of GO. For further analyses including supercapacitor application, they were dried by freeze drying method through removing of ice crystals to generate meso-pores inside the 3-D network for high surface area. FTIR spectra (Fig. 1 a) of the hydrogels confirm the reduction of oxygen functional groups (hydroxyl, carbonyl and oxirane ring) of the GO. Again, the graphitic (002) peak of GO in XRD pattern (Fig. 1b) shifted to 25° after formation of hydrogels, which further confirms the reduction of GO and formation of hydrogels by the π–π stacking interaction of graphene sheets under hydrothermal conditions [18]. All the major diffraction peaks of CuS in Fig. S2 are present in CuS-GOH and CuS@CD-GOH in Fig. 1b. The broadness of the graphitic (002) peaks of the hydrogels indicates the poor ordering of graphene sheets along their stacking direction [18]. The hydrogels were further analyzed by Raman study, as shown in Fig. 1c. The D and G bands of GO in the hydrogels were found around 1346 and 1586 cm−1, respectively. The D band is associated to the breathing mode of k-point phonons with A1g symmetry, while the G band is due to the 1st order scattering of the E2g phonon of sp2 carbon atoms. Therefore, the intensity ratio of D to G band (ID/IG) is usually employed to determine the degree of disorder for carbon material. From Fig. 1c, the ID/IG ratios of CuS-GOH (1.08) and CuS@CD-GOH (0.98) are found to be higher than pure GOH (0.96), which indicates lower disorders and defects of graphene sheets in presence of CuS as well as formation of new graphitic domains [48]. Due to the presence of CD, CuS@CD-GOH shows lower ID/IG ratio compared to CuS-GOH. The compositional analysis and the presence of the residual functional groups on the hydrogels were further investigated by XPS analysis. The survey spectrum of CuS@CD-GOH (Fig. 1d) shows the presence of C 1s (285 eV), O 1s (533 eV), Cu 2p (933 eV) and S 2p (163 eV), whereas, the spectrum of GOH shows the presence of only C 1s and O 1s. Deconvolution of C 1s and O 1s peaks (Fig. 2 a and b) reveal the presence of different residual functional groups in the hydrogel. Fig. 2c shows the presence of Cu 2p3/2 (933 eV) and Cu 2p1/2 (953 eV), which are typical Cu2+ peaks of CuS [48]. The high resolution spectrum (Fig. 2d) of S 2p shows the presence of S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 of a doublet peak at 163 eV, which is typical value of CuS.

The morphology of the hydrogels was investigated by FE-SEM analysis. Fig. 3 reveals the three-dimensional RGO nanosheets interconnected macroporous network. This kind of morphology attributed to the partial overlapping or coalescing of RGO sheets via supramolecular interactions such as π–π stacking and hydrogen bonding during hydrothermal reaction. The macropores were shaped by the ice crystals during freezing. Fig. 3b and c indicate the appearance of CuS and CuS@CD nanoparticles into the network through anchoring onto the surfaces of graphene sheets. Fig. 3b shows that a large number of big size CuS nanoparticles are aggregated and loosely attached to the surface of 3-D GO hydrogel. Whereas, Fig. 3c shows the tiny sized CuS@CD nanoparticles are homogeneously dispersed inside the 3-D hydrogel structure and are strongly attached to the graphene sheets. This is due to the presence of large numbers of surface functional groups on CD, which strongly interact with GO sheets during formation of hydrogel. High resolution FE-SEM image, Fig. 3d indicates that the CuS@CD spherical nanoparticles are strongly interconnected with edge functional groups of GO. Therefore, the CuS@CD particles are more homogeneously dispersed into the network compared to bare CuS. The energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) elemental mapping images of CuS@CD-GOH (Fig. 4 ) indicates all the compositional elements (C, O, Cu, S) are distributed homogeneously in the structural matrix. Fig. 4a–e represents the elemental color mapping images of the hydrogel by FE-SEM analysis. Fig. 4e confirms the homogeneous distribution of Cu and S elements on the C element, which indicates the homogeneous distribution of CuS nanoparticles on the surface of graphene sheets. This homogenous distribution of CuS in hydrogel structure was further investigated by STEM analysis (Fig. 4f–j). Fig. 4f represents the HAADF-STEM image of CuS@CD-GOH and Fig. 4g–j shows the corresponding elemental color mapping for C, O, Cu and S, respectively. All these images confirm that the Cu and S elements are well distributed inside the C matrix. TEM study was further used to investigate the internal structure of CuS@CD-GOH, as shown in Fig. 5 . The images demonstrate the crosslinked interconnected network structure with strongly attached spherical CuS@CD nanoparticles onto the surfaces of graphene sheets, which is consistent with the FE-SEM results. The HR-TEM images (Fig. 5c and d) show the strongly attached CuS@CD nanoparticle with graphene sheets, where two types of lattice fringes are found at 0.32 and 0.35 nm, which are corresponding to the crystallographic spacing d(102) of CuS and graphitic d(002) spacing of graphene, respectively. The porous nature of the hydrogels was further characterized by BET analysis. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of GOH, CuS-GOH and CuS@CD-GOH are shown in Fig. S4. The isotherms exhibited a hysteresis loop at a relative pressure between 0.45 and 1.0, which indicates the presence of mesopores and macropores inside the hydrogels [43]. The observed BET surface area was found to be 693, 354 and 514 m2 g−1 for GOH, CuS-GOH and CuS@CD-GOH, respectively. These results also suggest that the large numbers of surface functional groups on CD strongly interact with GO sheets during the formation of hydrogel. Therefore, BET surface area of CuS@CD-GOH is not much affected due to the homogeneous dispersion of CuS@CD particles into the 3-D structure. However, due to the aggregation and week interactions of bare CuS (as observed in FESEM study), the BET surface area of CuS-GOH is sharply decreased. This excellent specific surface area with porous structure is favorable for the electrochemical performance due to the facile ion transportation.

CV, GCD and EIS measurements were carried out to analyze the electrochemical performance of the hydrogels. Initially, the electrochemical performance of the hydrogels was analyzed through three electrode system using 6 M aq. KOH electrolyte. Fig. 6 a shows the comparative CV curves of GOH, CuS-GOH and CuS@CD-GOH (with the same wt% of loading of bare CuS and CuS@CD with respect to GO) at 25 mV s−1 scan rate. The figure clearly depicts that CuS@CD-GOH electrode based CV curve possess the largest area under the curve and therefore, it exhibits better supercapacitive performance compared to the other two hydrogels. The calculated (using Equation (1)) specific capacitance values for GOH, CuS-GOH and CuS@CD-GOH are found to be 120, 254, and 730 F g−1, respectively at 25 mV s−1. This enhanced supercapacitive property of the CuS@CD-GOH compared to CuS-GOH due to the presence of surface functional groups on CD facilitate the red-ox reaction of CuS and rise to an additional pseudo-capacitance contribution [26,27,29]. These functional groups of CD also bind the CuS nanoparticles with GO and homogeneously distribute them inside the porous 3-D GOH. This type of composite porous structure exhibits excellent specific surface area (as shown in BET analysis), which helps to reduce ion diffusion paths and therefore, enhance the electrocatalytic activity. Fig. 6b depicts the typical CV curves for the representative CuS@CD-GOH (50 wt% CuS@CD respect to GO) at different scan rate (20–100 mV s−1). The curves clearly show the pseudo-capacitance behavior with a pair of red-ox peaks for CuS in aqueous alkaline electrolyte [49,50], due to the red-ox reactions involved in CuS through the transitions between Cu(0), Cu(I) and Cu(II) species. The GCD curves (Fig. 6c) also show the voltage plateaus which are good agreement with the red-ox peaks observed in the CV curves. It exhibits capacity value 108.6 mA h g−1 at a current density of 1000 mA g−1. The specific capacitance values (calculated by using Equation (2)) of the electrode are found to be 782, 603, 547, 500, 451 and 410 F g−1 at current densities of 1, 2, 3, 5, 7 and 10 A g−1, respectively, as shown in Fig. 6d. Thus, it is seen that the retention of specific capacitance is around 53% at a high current density of 10 A g−1. This high rate capability of the electrode is due to the 3-D porous structure (as shown in FE-SEM image) with homogeneous distribution of CuS@CD nanohybrids, which reduces the diffusion resistance and facilitates ion and electron transport during the charge-discharge process at high current densities. The electrochemical performance of CuS@CD-GOH was also evaluated with the variation of amount of CuS@CD (25, 50 and 75 wt% with respect to GO) loadings inside the hydrogel structure. Fig. 6e shows that the specific capacitance value of the hydrogel was sharply increased with the increase of amount of CuS@CD nanohybrid. Without addition of the nanohybrid the hydrogel shows specific capacitance value of 128 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 current density, whereas with the addition of 25, 50 and 75 wt% of nanohybrid (with respect to GO), the specific capacitance of the hydrogel increase to 408, 782 and 920 F g−1 respectively, at the same current density of 1 A g−1. However, at the same time the cyclic stability of the electrode was decreased with the increase of amount of nanohybrid inside the hydrogel and it retained up to 76% after 5000 cycles at a current density of 5 A g−1 after incorporation of 75 wt% of CuS@CD nanohybrid, as shown in Fig. 6f. Whereas, with the incorporation of 25 and 50 wt% of nanohybrids the hydrogel shows cyclic stability of 95% and 87% after 5000 cycles at the same current density. Generally, reported CuS based supercapacitors exhibited <80% cyclic stability even after 1000–2000 cycles due to rapid volume change [36–38]. However, CuS@CD-GOH shows comparatively excellent cyclic stability due to the coating of CD on the surface of CuS, which help to bind strongly with GO sheets inside the 3-D hydrogel structure. Therefore, this surface coating and strong attachment with 3-D hydrogel structure prevent the volume change during electrochemical analysis and hence, enhanced the cyclic stability of CuS.

The 50 wt% CuS@CD nanohybrid decorated composite hydrogel was chosen for the fabrication of two electrode asymmetric device after balancing both the performance of specific capacitance and cyclic stability. The asymmetric supercapacitor device was fabricated utilizing CuS@CD-GOH as the positive electrode and RGO as the negative electrode using 6 M aq. KOH electrolyte inside a stainless steel point-cell, as reported in our earlier reports [43,44]. The CV curve of RGO shows a perfect EDLC at a stable potential window of 0 to −1.0 V as shown in Fig. 7 a. The electrochemical performance (CV and GCD analyses) of RGO using three-electrode system is shown in Fig. S5. The specific capacitance value of the RGO was found to be 125 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1 and it retains up to 67% (84 F g−1) specific capacitance at a high current density of 10 A g−1.

The electrochemical analyses of the asymmetric supercapacitor were measured with a stable potential window of 1.4 V. Fig. 7b shows the CV curves of the supercapacitor at different scan rates, 10–100 mV s−1. The couple of red-ox peaks can be observed from the curves, which indicates the pseudocapacitive characteristic of the supercapacitor. The current of the red-ox peaks continuously increases with the increase of the scan rate without distortion of the CV curves, which demonstrates the fast charge-discharge characteristic of the asymmetric supercapacitor [29]. In order to further investigate the performance of the supercapacitor GCD analysis was performed at different current densities, as shown in Fig. 7c. The GCD result shows good agreement with the CV analysis and the GCD curves also demonstrate sufficient coulombic efficiency (∼90%). The specific capacitance values of the asymmetric supercapacitor based on the total active mass of the two electrodes are calculated by using Equation (2). The calculated specific capacitance values at different current densities are shown in Fig. 7d. The calculated specific capacitance value for the asymmetric supercapacitor was found to be 103 F g−1 (capacity value, 40 mA h g−1) at a current density of 1 A g−1. It also retains up to 60% specific capacitance at a high current density of 10 A g−1, demonstrating a good rate performance. Again, energy and power densities are the key factors for the practical application of an asymmetric supercapacitor. The Ragone plot of the corresponding energy and power densities for the asymmetric supercapacitor is shown in Fig. 7e. The energy density and power density are calculated from the GCD curves by using Equations (4) And (5). The asymmetric supercapacitor delivers a high energy density of 28 W h kg−1 at a power density of 700 W kg−1. It maintains a high energy density up to 17 W h kg−1 even at a high power density of 7000 W kg−1. The obtained values are superior compared to the reported CuS as well as composite GO hydrogel based asymmetric supercapacitors [39,49,51–56]. The comparative electrochemical performance of the present asymmetric supercapacitor with different reported CuS and composite GO hydrogel based asymmetric supercapacitors is tabulated in Table 1 .

Further, for a high performance supercapacitor device with long cycling life is a critical requirement. Fig. 7f shows the cyclic stability of the asymmetric supercapacitor up to 5000 GCD cycles at a current density of 5 A g−1. The inset figure shows the 1st and 5000th GCD curves of the supercapacitor. It retains up to 90% specific capacitance value after 5000 cycles at a current density of 5 A g−1, which is higher compared to the reported CuS as well as composite GO hydrogel based asymmetric supercapacitors, as shown in Table 1. After 5000 cycles, the shape of the GCD curves remained same and also retained ∼100% coulombic efficiency. In order to further investigate the superior electrochemical performance and electron transfer kinetics, EIS measurement was performed at open circuit potential in the frequency range of 0.1 Hz–100 kHz. Fig. 8 shows the Nyquist plots of the asymmetric supercapacitor after 1st and 5000th GCD cycles. It shows a semicircle (inset of Fig. 8) in the high frequency region and a relatively straight line at low frequency. The x-intercept of the plot and the diameter of the semicircle represent the composed internal bulk resistance and charge transfer resistance of the electrode, respectively. The asymmetric supercapacitor shows very low internal resistance (0.76 Ω), which reveals low material, ionic and contact resistances of electrode/electrolyte interfaces due to the 3-D porous network with high surface area. The resistance remains low (1.0) after 5000 cycles, which further illustrates the excellent stability of the asymmetric device.

This study demonstrates a 3-D structure based on a CD-coated CuS-decorated GO hydrogel for using in high-performance supercapacitor applications. The structure exhibited a high specific capacitance, long cycling life, and high energy and power densities. The composite hydrogel, CuS@CD-GOH, was successfully fabricated through a facile hydrothermal reaction and was characterized using various techniques. The porous 3-D structure was determined with the FE-SEM analysis. The strong interactions between the CuS@CD nanohybrid and GO hydrogel, as well as the surface electronic state, were analyzed using TEM, FTIR, XPS and EDS analyses. The three-electrode electrochemical analysis of the composite hydrogel was performed with the variation of different doses of the CuS@CD nanohybrid inside the 3-D hydrogel. It showed up to 920 F g−1 specific capacitance value. The optimal hydrogel was used as positive electrode to fabricate asymmetric supercapacitor with reduced GO as the negative electrode. The device showed a high specific capacitance (103 F g−1) and cyclic stability (90%) while also delivering high energy and power densities. The performance obtained for the supercapacitor is superior compared to those reported for CuS and GO based supercapacitors. Therefore, this study successfully presents a porous 3-D structure to develop CuS and carbon based nanostructures for use in advanced energy storage applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by Basic Research Laboratory Program (2014R1A4A1008140) through the Ministry of Science, ICT & Future Planning of Korea and also was supported by the Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP) and the Ministry of Trade, Industry &Energy (MOTIE) of the Republic of Korea (No. 20164030201070).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

TEM and FESEM images of CuS@CD; TEM image of CuS; HRTEM image of CD; XRD patterns of CuS and CuS@CD; EDAX spectra of CuS and CuS@CD; N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of GOH, CuS-GOH and CuS@CD-GOH; and electrochemical performance of RGO.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2017.06.076.
